content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Ashlee N. Titus 
Bell, McAndrews & Hiltachk, LLP 
455 Capitol Mall, Suite 600 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-15-206 
Dear Ms. Titus: 
November 23, 2015 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Gail Robinette, a member of the 
Cambria Community Services District ("CCSD"), regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict 
of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as 
common law conflict of interest or Section I 090. Moreover, this letter is based on the facts 
presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders 
advice. (/11 re Oglesby ( 1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) 
QUESTIONS 
May Ms. Robinette participate in district decisions to: 
I. Activate a waitlist, on which her property is listed, for issuance of new water and sewer 
hookups needed to develop the properties? 
2. Re-open the waitlist, allowing new parcels to be added to the list? 
3. Amend the Water Master Plan to increase the maximum capacity for water and sewer 
hookups? 
4. Consider other water or development related issues? 
CONCLUSIONS 
l. No. The decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on 
Ms. Robinette's real property. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-15-206 
Page No. 2 
2 & 3. Yes. The decisions will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on 
her property. 
4. Without the factual context of a particular decision, we are unable to reach a conclusion 
regarding Ms. Robinette's participation in water or development related issues. 
FACTS 
Cambria is an unincorporated area in San Luis Obispo County that encompasses 
approximately 3,200 acres (5 square miles). Cambria is home to approximately 6,400 residents, 
with a substantial tourist and secondary home population. CCSD is an independent special district 
that provides sewer, water, wastewater, fire protection, lighting, refuse, parks, recreation and open 
space services and management. It is governed by a five-member board of directors, elected at­
large, to four-year staggered terms. Ms. Robinette has served on the board since December 20, 
2011. 
The county adopted a Growth Management Ordinance in 1990 that applies to Cambria as an. 
unincorporated community. In 2000, the County reduced Cambria's growth limit to 1 % each year, 
which corresponds to 8-12 new residential single-family homes annually. 
In light of Cambria's water shortage and the county's slow growth policy, a major issue that 
has long confronted CCSD is development of vacant lots (particularly residential) and allocation of 
existing and new water and sewer connections. In 1986, CCSD created a waitlist for new water and 
sewer hookups, essentially controlling the pace of development based on issuance of such 
connections. When the county adopted the Growth Management Ordinance, CCSD closed the 
waitlist to new applications ( effective December 31, l 990). CCSD also froze the waitlist and no 
new hookups have been issued since 2001. The single-family waitlist contains 666 parcels. Each 
parcel on the waitlist has a number, and should the waitlist ever be reactivated, connections would 
be issued according to the order on the waitlist. Ms. Robinette owns a vacant lot that is 136111 on the 
waitlist, with an estimated market value of$68,000. 
Under existing CCSD policy (subject to change if CCSD amends the Water Master Plan that 
was adopted in 2008), the maximum capacity for water and sewer connections in Cambria is 4,650. 
There are 3,900 residential lots that are already developed with water and sewer connections. There 
are approximately 1,000 additional "buildable" residential parcels that do not have a waitlist 
number. In 2013, CCSD voted to preserve the moratorium. 
If the waitlist is activated, the CCSD would start issuing a small number of "intent to serve" 
letters to provide a water connection to a specific parcel. In accordance with the county's Growth 
Management Ordinance, water conservation measures and other considerations, CCSD would vote 
to authorize 8-12 water connections per year to parcels on the waitlist, which would take 56-84 
years to move through the entire waitlist depending on the actual number authorized each year. At 
the rate of 8 per year, Ms. Robinette's number would come up in 17 years; at the rate of 12 per year, 
Ms. Robinette's number would come up in 12 years. 


File No. A-15-206 
Page No. 3 
Owners of property on the waitlist can obtain water hookups without activation of the list by 
using a procedure whereby the existing hookup from a developed parcel (the "sender parcel") is 
transferred to the property that's on the waitlist (the "receiver parcel.") For example, the owner of 
the receiver parcel could purchase the sender parcel, demolish any buildings on the sender parcel 
and '"transfer" the hookup to and build on the receiver parcel that was previously on the waitlist. 
The now vacant sender parcel is placed on the waitlist and assumes the number of the receiver 
parcel. Ms. Robinette was able to purchase such a property and build a home on the previously 
vacant lot by swapping the water connection and the waitlist number between her two properties. 
Should she decide to develop her vacant property, she could use the same process to obtain the 
necessary water and sewer hookups. 
Ms. Robinette presently has no plans to develop her vacant lot, even if the waitlist is 
activated. The lot is rather modest and unremarkable in comparison to other undeveloped lots in 
Cambria. Her property is the smallest buildable size under CCSD's existing building standards. Any 
structure that would be built would be limited to a maximum of 2,100 square feet and a carport, 
which is smaller than the typical Cambria home. The property does not have views of the ocean, 
hills or desirable open space, and is not in one of the coveted neighborhoods, but rather, in a mixed 
use, lower socio-economic neighborhood that includes multifamily housing and older construction. 
The market value of the lot is approximately $65,000. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
Section 87103 defines interests from which a conflict of interest may arise to include any real 
property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more. (Section 
87103(b).) 
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. The standard for foreseeability 
differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. 
Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is 
"explicitly involved" in a decision. An interest is "explicitly involved" in a decision if the interest is 
a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's 
agency. (Regulation l 870l(a).) Real property is the subject of a decision when "the decision 
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other 
entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision 
affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l )-(6). 
If the interest is "not explicitly involved" in the decision, a financial effect is reasonably 
foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical. 


File No. A-15-206 
Page No. 4 
I. Decision to activate the waitlist 
Foreseeability: 
The waitlist consists of residentially zoned undeveloped properties that are currently not 
entitled to water and sewer hookups that are needed in order to develop the property. Activating the 
waitlist would pennit. hookups, and ultimately, development of the property. A decision to activate 
the waitlist involves the "issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation" of a "license, permit, or 
other entitlement to, or contract with" her property. Consequently, a financial effect of the decision 
is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable. 
Materiality: 
Regulation 18702.2 provides the materiality standard for an interest in real property and lists 
multiple circumstances under which an effect on property will be considered material, including a 
decision that: 
"(5) Involves the issuance, denial or revocation of a license, pennit or 
other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use of or improvement to 
the parcel or any variance that changes the pennitted use of, or restrictions 
placed on, that real property .... ; 
* * * 
"(7) Would change the development potential of the parcel ofreal 
property; 
"(8) Would change the income producing potential of the parcel 
ofreal property. However, if the real property contains a business entity, 
including rental property, and the nature of the business entity remains 
unchanged, the materiality standards under Regulation 18705. l applicable 
to business entities would apply instead; 
"(9) Would change the highest and best use of the parcel of real property 
in which the official has a financial interest; 
* * * 
"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and 
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental 
decision was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would 
influence the market value of the official's property." 
Based on the facts presented, several of these materiality factors appear to apply to the decision's 
effects on Ms. Robinette's real property. 


File No. A-15-206 
Page No. 5 
In addition, you have suggested that the likelihood that Ms. Robinette or a future owner 
would build on the property bears on the question of foreseeability. She presently has no plans to 
build on the property and the property's location and characteristics make it less desirable for 
development than other undeveloped residential parcels in the city. However, even if Ms. Robinette 
has no intention to develop her lot, this is not an appropriate factor to consider in our analysis. The 
intended or probable use for property potentially benefited or harmed by a decision is not 
considered in the analysis of the reasonably foreseeable effects of a decision. The effect upon the 
value of the official's property, not whether the owner will act to realize the increased value by 
selling or developing the property, is the appropriate inquiry. (See In re Legan ( 1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 
1.) 
Under the facts you have provided, we find that the decision to activate the waitlist will 
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Ms. Robinette's real property and, 
therefore, she may not participate in the decision. 
2. Decision to re-open the waitlist. 
Ms. Robinette's property is not a named party in or the subject of the decision to re-open the 
waitlist. Therefore, her property is not explicitly involved in the decision. Under the foreseeability 
standard for real property not explicitly involved in a decision, any effect on her property is 
reasonably foreseeable only if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than 
hypothetical or theoretical. 
Ms. Robinette's property already has a waitlist number and any new parcels would be added 
to the end of the list. Even if the waitlist were reactivated and new parcels added, Ms. Robinette's 
property would retain its position on the waitlist, which would be higher than the added properties. 
Under these facts, any effect on her real property is, at best, theoretical and hypothetical and, 
therefore, not reasonably foreseeable. 
3. Decision to amend the Water Master Plan to allow more hookups. 
Ms. Robinette's property is not a named party in or the subject of the decision to amend the 
Water Master Plan to allow more hookups. Therefore, her property is not explicitly involved in the 
decision. Under the foreseeability standard for real property not explicitly involved in a decision, 
any effect on her property is reasonably foreseeable only if the effect can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. 
The Master Water Plan sets the maximum capacity for water and sewer connections in the 
city at 4,650. There are 3,900 residential lots that are already developed with connections, leaving 
750 hookups available for undeveloped lots. Ms. Robinette's property is number 136 on the waitlist 
and is reachable without increasing the maximum capacity for water and sewer connections in the 
Master Water Plan. Therefore, her property can be developed without amending the Water Master 
Plan. Under these facts, there is no reasonably foreseeable financial effect of the decision on her 
real property. 


File No. A-15-206 
Page No. 6 
4. Decisions to consider other water or development related issues. 
Each decision regarding water or development issues must be analyzed individually to 
determine whether the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of the decision on a public official's 
interests is material. You have not identified any such decisions with the specificity that our 
analysis would require. You may wish to request written advice from us in the future when you 
have more facts or a specific pending decision. 
VJ:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Valentina Joyce 
Counsel, Legal Division
